finding: §8.5 asserts a tapered clean round 'does not itself advance the count; §8.4 guarantees the next round is full-panel' — but §8.4 only forces the full panel when consecutive_clean >= 1 (or when a clean round would reach review_clean_rounds, i.e. review_clean_rounds=1). A tapered clean round leaves consecutive_clean at 0 (per §8.5 it did not advance), so with default review_clean_rounds=2 neither §8.4 clause fires and §8.4 provides NO guarantee. §9.5 computes overlap_ratio over 'total merged clusters' (not new/unresolved ones), so a clean round that re-surfaces already-resolved findings can still have overlap_ratio >= 0.5, causing §8.3 to taper the next round again. The panel can then stay reduced across successive clean rounds while §8.5 refuses to count any of them, so convergence is never declared and (with review_max_rounds set) the loop fails on a spec that is actually clean — a livelock in the flagship new feature.
suggestion: Make the safeguard self-contained: force the next round full-panel whenever the current round was clean (regardless of consecutive_clean/overlap), OR define overlap_ratio over new/unresolved clusters only so a clean round yields overlap 0.0 and never re-tapers. Correct §8.5 to cite the mechanism that actually guarantees the full panel.

finding: The taper base across consecutive tapered rounds is undefined. §8.3 drops 'the single lowest-yield reviewer' by reading round N-1's per_role_yield, but that map only contains roles EMITTED in N-1. If N-1 was already tapered (2 of 3 roles), does round N derive its panel from the full corpus-minus-one or the previous 2-role panel-minus-one? And does a role dropped in N-1 (absent from N-1's per_role_yield) ever return before a clean round resets to full? Two implementers will build monotonic-shrink vs recompute-from-full and get different panels.
suggestion: State explicitly that each tapered round's candidate set is the full domain-filtered corpus (not the previous panel), that the single drop is recomputed each round, and whether previously-dropped roles are eligible to return; or define the panel as a running set that only grows back on a full-panel round.

finding: Location normalization is not implementable for findings without a §-token. It says fall back to 'the nearest preceding spec heading id', but (a) tp specs use '## N. Title' headings that have no 'id' — slug? numeric prefix? raw text? undefined; and (b) 'nearest preceding' presupposes a POSITION in the spec, yet a finding's location is free text (e.g. 'line 27', "Table '3. Role Definition Schema'", 'Non-Goals item 4'). The spec never says how to extract a spec position (a line number?) from arbitrary location text, so the fallback cannot be coded deterministically.
suggestion: Define 'heading id' concretely (e.g. the leading '§N.N.N' derived from the numbered heading), and specify the extraction rule for the no-§ case: e.g. parse a trailing 'line N' integer and map to the enclosing '## N.' heading, and specify what happens when no line number and no § is present (own cluster).

finding: Whether a non-empty phase directory REPLACES or is MERGED with the embedded default corpus is never stated, yet it governs every emission. §4.4 says only that an empty/absent directory uses the embedded corpus; it does not say a directory with one file suppresses the rest of the embedded corpus. An implementer could reasonably union embedded + user roles. §5.3/§5.4 (eject the whole set, then edit) imply replace-semantics, but that inference is load-bearing and unspecified — the difference is a 1-role panel vs a 4-role panel.
suggestion: State explicitly: 'A non-empty phase directory fully replaces the embedded corpus for that phase; embedded and user roles are never merged. To customize a default, eject it first.'

finding: Across consecutive non-clean rounds (findings present, overlap >= 0.5, consecutive_clean stays 0 so §8.4 does not force a full panel), §8.3 says tp 'drops the single lowest-yield reviewer from round N' but never defines the candidate panel for round N. Is it (a) always the full corpus with exactly one drop, or (b) the previous round's already-reduced panel with one MORE drop (cumulative shrink)? The MIN_DIVERSITY_ROLES=1 floor only ever bites under interpretation (b) for a 3-role software corpus, implying (b) is intended — yet §8.6 stores `per_role_yield` only for EMITTED roles, so a role dropped in N-1 has no yield entry to rank in N under either reading. I cannot write the test `given rounds 1 and 2 both non-clean with overlap 0.6, assert round 3's emitted panel` — the two interpretations give 2 roles vs 1 role.
suggestion: Specify whether taper is cumulative or a fresh single-drop from the full corpus each round, and define how a role absent from round N-1's per_role_yield map is ranked (treat as yield 0? re-include at full panel?).

finding: §9.2 produces two different anchor namespaces: the §-token path yields a string like '§5.1', while the fallback yields a 'spec heading id' — whose format is itself undefined (the number '5'? the slug 'default-corpus-eject'? the full '## 5. Default Corpus & Eject'?). Because clustering requires normalized locations to be EQUAL, two findings citing the same section via different string forms (one writing '§5.1', another writing 'section 5.1' with no § symbol) normalize to non-equal keys and never merge — defeating the overlap measurement the whole taper depends on. I cannot write the test `location="§5.1" and location="5.1 corpus" cluster together` because the two normalization paths emit incomparable key formats.
suggestion: Canonicalize both paths to a single key space (e.g. always the dotted section number '5.1', stripping the § and any heading text), and give the exact string produced for each input form.

finding: The convergence safeguard hinges on a per-round `full_panel` boolean (§8.5 gates `consecutive_clean` on it; §8.6 persists it), but the spec never defines how `full_panel` is COMPUTED. Is it `emitted_roles == full domain-filtered corpus`, `emitted_roles == raw corpus`, or simply `this round was not tapered`? These differ when domain filtering already excludes roles, and when the corpus is a single role (taper cannot fire, so is the panel trivially 'full'?). I cannot write the test `assert full_panel == true for round N` because the predicate has no definition — two testers would encode different equalities.
suggestion: State the exact rule, e.g. `full_panel := (emitted diversity roles == the domain-filtered corpus, ignoring the built-in regression role)`, and give the value for a 1-role corpus and for a domain-filtered corpus explicitly.

finding: §4.4 falls back to the embedded corpus only when a phase directory is 'absent or empty' (empty == no *.json). §6.2 then filters the present user files by domain. Nothing specifies the outcome when user role files EXIST but every one is filtered out by its `domains` (e.g. `.tp/reviewers/` holds one file with domains:["prose"] and the spec's domain is software): the directory is non-empty so there is no embedded fallback, yet the active diversity panel is empty. Does tp error, emit regression-only, or fall back to embedded? I cannot write the test `tp review with all reviewers domain-filtered out emits <?>`.
suggestion: State the behavior for a non-empty phase whose domain-filtered active set is empty — e.g. hard error with a hint, or emit regression only — and whether it differs from the absent/empty-directory embedded fallback.

finding: §5.2 states the built-in `regression` reviewer 'cannot be ejected or overridden' and 'its prompt is tp's existing regression prompt.' But §11.4 says the legacy `lens.all` shim 'appends to every active review role's focus (and regression)', and §11.2's override mechanism (which the shim translates into) appends `focus` questions keyed by role id — nothing excludes a direct `tp.review_roles: { regression: { focus: [...] } }`. Since this spec calls appending focus an 'override' (§11.1 item 3, §11.3), §5.2 and §11.2/§11.4 directly contradict on whether regression's prompt is mutable. I cannot write the test `tp.review_roles.regression.focus is appended to the regression prompt` vs `regression override is rejected/ignored` — both are specified.
suggestion: Decide one way: either regression accepts appended focus (then remove 'cannot be overridden' from §5.2 and say lens.all/overrides append to it), or it is immutable (then state that §11.2/§11.4 skip regression and lens.all does NOT touch it).

finding: §5.2 states the built-in regression reviewer 'cannot be ejected or overridden' and has a fixed prompt. But §11.4's back-compat shim says legacy 'lens.all appends to every active review role's focus (and regression)' — explicitly extending regression — and §11.2's spec override map is keyed by role id with no stated exclusion of regression (nor a focus slot, since regression is not a corpus file with a focus field). Appending focus questions to regression IS overriding it. §5.2 and §11.2/§11.4 directly contradict on whether regression's prompt is mutable.
suggestion: Decide one way: either regression accepts appended focus (then §5.2 must drop 'cannot be overridden' and define where regression's base focus lives), or it does not (then §11.4 must exclude regression from lens.all and §11.2 must reject/ignore an override id of regression with a warning).

finding: §10.1 hashes 'builtin' as a fixed sentinel when a phase has no user files, and a real sha256 once files exist; §10.2 marks convergence stale on any hash change. Running `tp init --eject-roles` writes the default corpus to disk as byte-for-byte the same prompts, but flips the hash from 'builtin' to a sha256 — so ejecting silently marks review AND/OR audit convergence stale even though the emitted prompts are identical. This contradicts §5.4, which frames ejecting as behavior-neutral inspection that 'carries no maintenance burden.' A team that ejects purely to read the prompts unexpectedly loses its converged state.
suggestion: Either exclude an ejected-but-unmodified default corpus from staleness (e.g. hash the resolved prompt content so byte-identical defaults hash to the same value as 'builtin'), or state explicitly in §5.4/§10 that eject invalidates convergence and is therefore not behavior-neutral.

finding: When findings omit `class` the entire §8 taper goes inert, and this is not acknowledged. §9.3 keeps every empty-class finding as its own singleton cluster; §9.5's overlap ratio denominator = TOTAL clusters (including those unmergeable singletons); and §8.2 yield counts clusters whose found_by_roles is a single role. Since `class` is optional in the finding contract and nothing in §7.3 emission requires it, a round where agents don't stamp class produces near-zero found_by>=2 clusters -> overlap_ratio ~ 0 (< 0.5 threshold) and every role reads as high-yield -> taper never fires. The feature silently depends on class being populated but never requires it.
suggestion: Either require `class` in the emitted output contract (§7.3) and specify record behavior when it is missing, or compute the overlap ratio over only class-bearing clusters (exclude empty-class singletons from the denominator) so the ratio measures real overlap.

finding: Attribution assumes every input finding carries a valid `role`, but nothing validates it and the failure modes are undefined. §7.3 only INSTRUCTS the sub-agent (an LLM) to stamp its role id; an agent may omit `role`, stamp an empty string, or stamp an id not in the emitted panel (hallucinated/renamed). §9.4 says found_by_roles is 'the sorted set of distinct contributing roles' with no rule for missing/unknown roles, so a missing role silently deflates found_by (breaking overlap) and a phantom role injects a bogus per_role_yield entry (breaking §8.3's 'lowest-yield' ranking).
suggestion: Define merge/record behavior for findings with absent/empty `role` (drop? bucket as 'unknown'? error?) and for a `role` not in the round's emitted panel (ignore for yield? warn?). Specify that per_role_yield keys are restricted to emitted_roles.

finding: The roles_hash path component makes the hash nondeterministic across clones. §10.1 hashes 'each file's path and content' with files 'sorted by path', but does not say whether 'path' is absolute, repo-root-relative, or .tp/-relative. If absolute (the naive filepath.Walk result), the hash embeds '/Users/alice/...' vs '/home/bob/...', so a teammate who clones to a different directory computes a different review_roles_hash and §10.4 reports roles_stale=true for an unchanged corpus — defeating the whole 'travels with the repo' goal (§4.3).
suggestion: Specify the hashed path is relative to the repo-root .tp/ (e.g. 'reviewers/security.json') and that sorting is byte/codepoint-wise on that relative path. Confirm the extension/stem is included as authored.

finding: §9.2's fallback normalization — 'absent a §<n> token, the nearest preceding spec heading id' — is not computable from a finding, because a finding's `location` is a free-text string with no line position in the spec; 'nearest preceding heading' presumes a position that findings do not carry. Worse, when a location has neither a §-token nor any derivable heading (e.g. location='the coverage table' or 'general'), §9.2 yields no anchor and §9.3 gives a bucketing rule only for empty CLASS, not for an unnormalizable LOCATION. I cannot write the test `finding with location="the coverage table", class="x" clusters into <?>` — the normalized key and whether it is dropped, self-clustered, or errored are all undefined.
suggestion: Define fallback purely in terms of the location string (e.g. 'if no §-token, take the first bare N.N.N run; if none, the finding is its own singleton cluster'), and state the singleton/keep rule for unnormalizable locations mirroring §9.3's empty-class rule.

finding: §8.1 justifies the entire taper apparatus by token savings 'once a spec stabilizes,' but §8.4 forces the full panel exactly when the spec stabilizes (consecutive_clean >= 1). So taper can only ever operate during the active-finding middle — precisely when last round's per-role yield is the worst predictor of this round's unique finds and dropping a reviewer is riskiest, and when reviewers are genuinely NOT redundant (they are still surfacing new issues). The machinery it buys this with is heavy: 4 new persisted per-round fields (emitted_roles, full_panel, per_role_yield, overlap_ratio), 3 named constants, counterfactual emission logic, and a migration path. Against the RUTHLESS-MINIMALISM / Principle-4 bar, the stated need does not earn this apparatus: it is disabled in the regime it was justified for and dangerous in the regime it runs in.
suggestion: Either re-justify §8 against the regime it actually runs in (mid-stream high-overlap), or cut it to the minimal earned slice (e.g. drop only roles with zero yield across ALL prior rounds, no per-round overlap threshold), or defer §8 entirely until dogfooding demonstrates the token waste it claims to fix.

finding: roles_hash storage granularity is contradictory. §8.6 enumerates exactly four new PER-ROUND fields (emitted_roles, full_panel, per_role_yield, overlap_ratio) and pointedly omits any roles_hash, implying it is top-level. But §10 staleness parallels spec_hash, which is stored per-round on ReviewRound and compared last-round-vs-current by StateStale. §10.2 only says 'stored in state.json' without saying per-round or which round the current corpus hash is compared against. Implementers will fork on top-level-field vs per-round-field, changing what 'stale' means after a mid-loop role edit.
suggestion: State explicitly whether review_roles_hash/audit_roles_hash are per-round entries (recorded at each --record, compared last-vs-current like spec_hash) or a single top-level field, and add them to the §8.6 field list if per-round.

finding: Ejected role files' `domains` fidelity is unspecified. The embedded corpus is grouped by domain COLUMNS in the §5.1 table (implying the grouping lives in tp, not in a per-role field), but §6.2 filters the ACTIVE corpus by each role's `domains` field. After `tp init --eject-roles --domain prose`, do the written coherence.json/soundness.json carry `domains:['prose']`? If eject omits the field, the ejected roles have no `domains` and therefore apply to EVERY domain (§6.2) — so a later software spec would emit the prose reviewers.
suggestion: Specify that --eject-roles writes an explicit `domains` field on each ejected role reflecting the domain column it came from (roles shared across columns get all applicable domains or the field omitted intentionally), so re-read filtering reproduces the pre-eject panel.

finding: The accepted boolean token set is undefined. §13.1 says parsing accepts 'true / false, rejecting other values with exit 1', but Go's obvious primitive strconv.ParseBool also accepts 1,0,t,f,T,F,TRUE,True,FALSE,False. Whether `tp set --workflow review_adaptive=1` or `=TRUE` is valid, and whether matching is case-sensitive, is unspecified — two implementers will diverge (strict lowercase literals vs ParseBool).
suggestion: Enumerate the exact accepted tokens (e.g. only lowercase 'true'/'false', case-insensitive, or Go ParseBool set) for both `tp set --workflow`/`--project` string input and the config.json JSON value, and give the exact error text for rejects.

finding: §8.4's second condition ('emit full corpus whenever a clean round N would reach review_clean_rounds') evaluates at emission to `consecutive_clean == review_clean_rounds - 1`. For any review_clean_rounds >= 2 this is subsumed by the first clause (`consecutive_clean >= 1`), so the clause is inert. It only adds behavior at review_clean_rounds == 1, where it fires at consecutive_clean == 0 and thereby forces a full panel on EVERY round — silently disabling adaptive tapering entirely. Neither the redundancy nor the review_clean_rounds==1 disabling is stated. I cannot write the test `with review_adaptive=true and review_clean_rounds=1, tapering occurs on round 2` because the spec implies it never does but does not say so.
suggestion: Either drop the redundant second clause, or explicitly state that review_clean_rounds==1 forces a permanent full panel (taper disabled) and add that boundary case to the examples.

finding: The back-compat shim is under-specified on three axes needed to test it: (a) does `lens.all` affect AUDIT roles, or only review roles + regression as literally written? (b) When a legacy key names no active role (e.g. `lens.implementer` under the prose corpus), does it emit only the deprecation warning, or the deprecation warning PLUS §11.2's unmatched-override lint warning, or an error? (c) 'every active review role' — is 'active' the domain-filtered corpus or the post-taper emitted set for a given round? I cannot write deterministic tests for a legacy `tp: lens` block's effect on audit prompts, its warning output, or its interaction with a tapered round.
suggestion: Specify that lens.all maps to review roles + regression only (never audit), define the warning set for an unmatched legacy key, and define 'active' as the domain-filtered corpus (pre-taper) for override attachment.

finding: per_role_yield must be zero-filled over emitted_roles, but the spec never says so. A role that was emitted yet produced zero findings contributes to no cluster, so a naive 'map id->int built from clusters' omits it entirely. §8.3's 'single lowest-yield reviewer' then cannot see the zero-yield role and would drop a role with yield 1 instead of the one that found nothing — the exact opposite of the intent.
suggestion: State that per_role_yield is keyed over emitted_roles with absent contributors set to 0, and that §8.3 ranks over emitted_roles using that zero-filled map (regression excluded).

finding: Domain filtering can empty the diversity panel with no defined floor or error. MIN_DIVERSITY_ROLES=1 is a TAPER floor (§8.3), not an emission floor. A custom corpus whose every role declares `domains` excluding the spec's domain (e.g. all roles domains:['software'] but spec domain=prose) filters down to zero active reviewers, leaving only the built-in regression prompt. The spec never says whether an empty post-filter panel is an error, a warning, or a silent single-prompt review.
suggestion: Define behavior when domain filtering yields zero active roles: at minimum a lint/emit warning ('no reviewer role matches domain X'), and decide whether regression-only emission is acceptable or an error.

finding: §9.4 asserts 'every input finding carries a `role` (stamped per §7.3)', and yield/overlap depend on it, but §7.3 only says the prompt INSTRUCTS the sub-agent to stamp — a non-compliant or truncated sub-agent can return a finding with a missing or blank `role`. The merge behavior for such a row (dropped, assigned a sentinel role, or hard error) is unspecified, and the change to found_by/found_by_roles/yield/overlap is therefore undefined. Additionally `role` is not shown as a field in the finding NDJSON schema (the documented row is severity/category/location/finding/suggestion + optional class), so its required/optional status is unstated. I cannot write the test `--merge on a row with no role field -> cluster.found_by_roles == <?>`.
suggestion: Add `role` to the finding NDJSON schema as required, and specify §9's handling of a missing/blank role stamp (reject the row with an exit code, or bucket under a reserved 'unknown' role).

finding: A spec-frontmatter override value is defined as `{ focus: [string...] }`, but the spec does not say what happens when an override value carries additional keys (e.g. `{ focus:[...], instructions:"x" }` or `{ title:"y" }`). §3.4's unknown-key rejection applies to ROLE FILES, not frontmatter overrides. Is an extra key rejected (exit 1), ignored, or applied? I cannot write the test `tp.review_roles.tester.instructions is rejected/ignored` because §11.2 defines only the allowed key, not the treatment of disallowed ones.
suggestion: State that override values MUST contain only `focus`, and that any other key is a lint warning (or error), mirroring §3.4's stance for role files.

finding: The safeguard's second clause ('tp emits the full corpus ... whenever a clean round N would reach review_clean_rounds') silently disables tapering entirely when review_clean_rounds=1 (a valid value, range 1-10). With review_clean_rounds=1 every round at consecutive_clean=0 could be the converging clean round, so every round must be full-panel — and tapering only ever runs at consecutive_clean=0. Thus §8's headline mechanism becomes inert for that config with no note. For review_clean_rounds=2 the clause is fully redundant with the first (consecutive_clean>=1).
suggestion: Acknowledge and specify the review_clean_rounds=1 interaction (either accept that adaptive scaling is a no-op there, or make the safeguard force full-panel only when consecutive_clean>=1). Note the clause is redundant for review_clean_rounds>=2.

finding: The migration shim appends focus to the built-in `regression` role, which §5.2 gives no focus slot. §11.4 says 'lens.all appends to every active review role's focus (and regression)', and §11.2 overrides target any 'active role' (regression is always active). But §5.2 defines regression only as 'tp's existing regression prompt' — a built-in, not a §3.3 role file with a `focus` array. Where appended focus questions go in the regression prompt, and whether §11.2 `tp.review_roles.regression` is even allowed, is undefined.
suggestion: State whether the built-in regression prompt has an appendable focus section (and where injected), and whether spec overrides / legacy lens may target `regression`; if not, exclude regression from focus-append and say so.

finding: §5.2 says a `.tp/reviewers/regression.json` file 'is a validation error (§3.6)', but §3.6's enumerated validation checks are only: parses, has required fields, id==filename stem, id matches the kebab pattern, domains is a string array, no unknown keys. A file `regression.json` with `{id:"regression",...}` passes ALL of these (stem is 'regression', pattern matches). A validator built strictly from §3.6 would ACCEPT it, contradicting §5.2. I cannot write a §3.6-conformant validator test that rejects regression.json because §3.6 lists no reserved-id check.
suggestion: Add a reserved-id rule to §3.6's enumerated checks: 'id MUST NOT be a reserved built-in id (`regression`) in the reviewers phase' — so the validation list actually contains the rule §5.2 references.

finding: §3.6 lists `--eject-roles` among the 'role-reading commands' that a malformed role file 'aborts ... with exit 3.' §5.3 says eject overwrites an existing role file when `--force` is given. These collide when a MALFORMED role file already exists at a target path and the user runs `tp init --eject-roles --force`: §3.6 demands an exit-3 abort (the file is unparseable), while §5.3's --force is meant to overwrite exactly such an existing file. I cannot write the test `--eject-roles --force over a corrupt existing role.json -> overwrite vs exit 3`.
suggestion: State precedence explicitly: e.g. `--force` overwrites regardless of the existing file's validity (skip the parse-abort for files it is about to replace), or --eject-roles validates the whole corpus first and never repairs via overwrite.

finding: §3.5 claims the word 'lens' is now reserved for exactly two things — the retired frontmatter (§11) and the general 'failure-lens' concept — to justify renaming the schema field to `focus`. But the rewrite still uses 'lens' loosely as a synonym for role/perspective elsewhere: Principle 2 (§2.2) 'the user owns the input lens,' and §6.3 'additional lenses mostly overlap' / 'its lenses genuinely diverge.' These are neither the retired frontmatter nor the failure-lens concept, so §3.5's reservation claim is not actually honored — the collision the rename was meant to remove persists at the prose level.
suggestion: Either narrow §3.5's claim to acknowledge 'lens' also survives as the informal failure-mode synonym, or scrub §2.2/§6.3 to say 'input customization' / 'reviewer perspectives' so 'lens' is truly confined to §11 and §16's failure-lens term.

finding: §5.2 asserts a `.tp/reviewers/regression.json` file is a validation error and attributes that rule to §3.6, but §3.6's enumerated validator checks (parses, required fields, id==filename-stem, id matches pattern, domains is a string array, no unknown keys) contain no reserved-id rejection. A well-formed regression.json (id 'regression', valid fields) passes every §3.6 check, so the cited section does not actually establish the rule §5.2 relies on.
suggestion: Add the reserved-id check to §3.6's validation list (e.g. 'the reserved id `regression` (§5.2) in `.tp/reviewers/` is rejected'), or drop the '(§3.6)' citation and have §5.2 state the reserved-id validation rule directly.

finding: §8.5 states '§8.4 guarantees the next round is full-panel' after a clean tapered round. But §8.4's full-panel triggers are `consecutive_clean >= 1` OR a clean round reaching `review_clean_rounds`. Per §8.5 a clean tapered round does NOT advance `consecutive_clean`, so after such a round the count is still 0; with the default `review_clean_rounds`=2 neither §8.4 trigger fires, so round N+1 may taper again — §8.4 does not provide the guarantee §8.5 cites. The overall safety invariant survives via §8.5's own counting rule (only full_panel clean rounds advance the count), but the inter-section guarantee claim is inaccurate and could mislead an implementer into omitting the counting-based guard.
suggestion: Reword §8.5 to rest the 'never declared on a reduced panel' conclusion on its own counting rule (only full_panel clean rounds advance `consecutive_clean`) rather than asserting §8.4 forces the following round full-panel; alternatively extend §8.4 to force a full panel in the round immediately after any clean tapered round so the cited guarantee actually holds.

finding: Clustering is fragile against the §-glyph. '§8.2' normalizes to the anchor §8.2, but '8.2' or 'sec 8.2' or 'Section 8.2' (no literal § character) has 'no § token' and falls to the heading-fallback path, so two findings about the identical location fail to cluster purely because one author typed the § glyph and another did not. Sub-agents (LLMs) are highly inconsistent about the § character, which directly corrupts found_by/overlap and therefore the taper.
suggestion: Normalize the anchor tolerant of the § glyph: match /§?\s*<n>(\.<n>)*/ with an optional 'Section'/'sec' prefix, and lowercase/strip so '§8.2', '8.2', 'Section 8.2' all map to the same anchor.

finding: The pipeline that computes per_role_yield and overlap_ratio is unspecified, and today's --record just writes the file it is handed verbatim (it does not re-cluster). §8.2/§9.4 define yield/overlap over MERGED clusters carrying found_by_roles, but a user may run `tp review <spec> --record raw-per-role.ndjson` (skipping --merge). If --record trusts pre-merged input, raw findings have no found_by_roles -> per_role_yield/overlap_ratio are empty and the taper misbehaves silently; if --record is supposed to re-run §9 clustering internally, that is never stated.
suggestion: Specify where §9 clustering runs for state recording: either mandate that --record's input is the §9-merged file (and define behavior/warn when found_by_roles is absent), or state that --record re-clusters its raw input to derive per_role_yield/overlap_ratio.

finding: §10.1 computes each phase hash as sha256 over the phase's user files 'sorted by path, hashing each file's path and content,' but does not state whether 'path' is absolute, CWD-relative, or repo-relative. If absolute or CWD-relative paths enter the hash, the same committed corpus hashes differently on a different checkout/machine, so §4.3's requirement that 'convergence enforcement must travel with the repo' breaks: every clone would see roles_stale. spec_hash (which this is modeled against) hashes content only.
suggestion: Specify the path component is repo-relative (relative to the .tp/ or .git anchor), or drop path from the hash and hash filename stem + content, so the hash is stable across checkouts.

finding: §9.2 normalizes a finding's location to 'its leading §<n> token, or — absent that — the nearest preceding spec heading id.' The fallback presupposes the location can be resolved to a position within the spec, but a free-form location string (e.g. 'the merge step', 'taper rule') carries no line number or inherent spec position, so 'nearest preceding heading' has no defined anchor to search from. The fallback is undefined for precisely the free-form locations it exists to handle, so those findings cannot be clustered deterministically.
suggestion: Either require every finding's location to begin with a §anchor or an exact heading id (validated at merge), or define the fallback concretely (e.g. locations without a resolvable anchor each form their own singleton cluster, like the empty-class rule in §9.3).

finding: §8.6 adds full_panel (and the other taper fields) to each recorded review round, and §8.5 advances consecutive_clean 'only if full_panel is true.' Rounds recorded before v0.25.0 have no full_panel field. The spec never states how a missing full_panel is interpreted on upgrade. If it defaults to false (the Go zero value), an already-converged project is silently un-converged by the upgrade and forced through fresh full-panel rounds. §15/§10.1's 'builtin' sentinel protects against embedded-prompt drift but does NOT cover this vector.
suggestion: State that a recorded round lacking full_panel is treated as full_panel=true (pre-v0.25.0 always ran the full panel), so upgrading preserves existing convergence. Add this to §15 Migration.

finding: Two mechanization signals now coexist with no combination rule. §9.6 adds a found_by>=2 candidate that 'appears in --merge and --report output alongside the existing class-frequency candidates' (computeMechanizeCandidates today). The same class can be surfaced by BOTH signals, and the spec does not say whether they are merged into one list, deduped by class, or emitted as two separate arrays — so output shape and dedup are undefined.
suggestion: Specify how the found_by>=2 candidates combine with the existing class-frequency candidates: one deduped list keyed by class (with a reason/source field), or two clearly-named arrays.

finding: A spec carrying BOTH a new-style `tp.review_roles` override and a legacy `tp: lens` block for the same role id has no defined combination. §11.4 auto-translates lens into §11.2-style overrides with a deprecation warning; §11.3 layers project ⊕ override. When both override sources target the same role, the order/whether both append is unspecified (does lens.all + review_roles.<id> both append, and in what order relative to project focus?).
suggestion: Define precedence/ordering when both the legacy lens shim and new-style tp.review_roles/tp.audit_roles overrides are present for the same role, or state that presence of tp.review_roles suppresses the legacy shim.

finding: The default software auditor corpus (§5.1) ships `maintainability-conventions`, a single role bundling two distinct concerns (maintainability + conventions). §16.2 principle 1 requires 'Role = one distinct failure-lens' and §16.4 states the embedded defaults are authored to exemplify that guidance. A default role that packs two lenses into one id undercuts the exemplar it is supposed to be.
suggestion: Split into `maintainability` and `conventions`, or rename to a single coherent lens, so the shipped defaults match the one-lens-per-role guidance they are meant to demonstrate.

finding: Unknown-domain handling is asymmetric: an unknown spec-frontmatter tp.domain (§6.1) falls back to the software corpus with a lint warning (soft), while an unknown `--eject-roles --domain` (§5.3) is a hard usage error (exit 2). The asymmetry may be intentional (data vs explicit CLI intent) but is not stated as such, inviting confusion about which path a user hits.
suggestion: Add one sentence noting the asymmetry is deliberate — frontmatter data must not break the pipeline, an explicit CLI flag should fail loud — or unify the two behaviors.
